title: Thermodynamic-limit dispersion relations on trapped-ion quantum hardware
authors: Lucas Marti, Sumeet, Stefan Wolf, K. P. Schmidt, Michael J. Hartmann
abstract: We run a numerical linked-cluster expansion with a quantum algorithm (NLCE+QA), computing ground-state energies and one quasi-particle dispersions in the thermodynamic limit using a 20-qubit trapped-ion quantum processing unit (QPU). The NLCE+QA framework extracts thermodynamic-limit properties from small-cluster calculations, making it naturally suited for near-term quantum devices. Projector-based block-diagonalization schemes such as projective cluster-additive transformation (PCAT) are essential to NLCE+QA, and they involve matrix inversion and square root operations that amplify measurement noise. A central question is therefore whether current hardware can provide expectation values that are accurate enough to withstand non-linear classical post-processing. We explore this challenge for the transverse-field Ising model (TFIM) in one dimension, on a ladder geometry, as well as in a longitudinal field in one dimension. For the quantum algorithm, we consider adiabatic state preparation (ASP), as well as a variational quantum eigensolver (VQE) trained on a classical device. The final expectation values are obtained from the QPU, using a novel alternative to the Hadamard test that we name the CX-test. We explore the regimes currently attainable on quantum devices and comment on the improvements needed for quantum computers to achieve results beyond classical reach.
summary: This paper explores using a 20-qubit trapped-ion processor to compute many-body dispersion relations in the thermodynamic limit via a hybrid quantum-classical expansion technique. It demonstrates that while current hardware can successfully implement the necessary non-linear transformations, noise significantly biases the results and necessitates much higher gate fidelities for more complex algorithms like ASP.
main_problem: Determining if current noisy quantum hardware can accurately compute thermodynamic-limit dispersion relations using non-linear classical post-processing of quantum data.
main_result: The study demonstrates that the NLCE+VQE framework is viable on current trapped-ion hardware, producing recognizable dispersion relations, though noise significantly biases results and ASP requires much lower error rates to be competitive.
method: The authors combine Numerical Linked-Cluster Expansion (NLCE) with quantum algorithms (VQE and ASP) and use a novel CX-test for efficient measurement of matrix elements.
model_or_system: The study uses a 20-qubit AQT Marmot trapped-ion quantum processing unit to simulate the Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) in 1D chains, 1D chains with a longitudinal field, and 2D ladders.
key_observables: Ground-state energies and one-quasi-particle (1QP) dispersion relations omega(k).
important_parameters: Coupling strength J/h, longitudinal field strength hl, cluster size Nmax, and gate depolarization rates.
main_assumptions: The perturbation is small enough to avoid quantum phase transitions or quasi-particle decays, and the hardware error is primarily modeled as depolarizing noise.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the HVA ansatz, the CX-test circuit, the impact of depolarization noise on dispersion curves, and comparisons between QPU, simulation, and analytical results for various TFIM regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the NLCE+QA framework, details the PCAT post-processing and CX-test measurement technique, compares VQE and ASP algorithms, analyzes the impact of hardware noise and stability, and concludes with the requirements for reaching the classical limit.
why_interesting: It addresses the critical challenge of how error propagation through non-linear classical post-processing affects the extraction of many-body properties from NISQ-era quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2. The CX-test. In the second register, the controlled X gates indices indicate which qubit it is applied to, out of the N qubits.

Figure 3. Interplay between shot noise and depolarization noise when computing the dispersion curve, using noisy simulation, for a TFIM chain at J/h = 0.8 obtained by NLCE with Nmax = 5. The Y -axis label shows the number of shots, while the X-axis label shows the one-qubit and the two-qubit gate depolarization rates, indicated by 1q and 2q. The red line represents the noiseless statevector simulation, and the green line a noisy simulation. The error bars denote one standard deviation of statistical uncertainty.
Figure 4. Number of unique circuits to compute the full NLCE+QA scheme, for the pure TFIM model on the chain and on the square lattice (using rectangular expansion for NLCE).

Figure 5. 1QP Dispersion for the one-dimensional TFIM chain in the thermodynamic limit for J/h = 0.3 using NLCE+VQE with Nmax = 5. NCLE+ED indicates NLCE performed with exact diagonalization as a cluster solver; it shows the best possible performance of NLCE when using these cluster sizes. Analytic labels the analytic solution of the one-dimensional TFIM model, given in Eq. (25). SV in- dicates a noiseless statevector simulation. Noisy indicates a simulation with gate-level depolarization noise with rates set to be close to an AQT device. QPU indicates an experimen- tal result on a 20-qubit AQT trapped-ion device. The error bars denote one standard deviation of statistical uncertainty.

Figure 6. 1QP dispersion for the one-dimensional TFIM model in the thermodynamic limit at J/h = 0.3, obtained with NLCE+ASP. The results take Nmax = 5 in NLCE cal- culation, and the sweep is done in ten time steps. Labels are the same as in Fig. 5. The error bars denote one standard deviation of statistical uncertainty.
